definition: The Grail is the sacred vessel of Arthurian legend — the object of the great quest, identified with the cup of the Last Supper. It is defined less by what it is than by its being sought: unlike an ordinary *chalice*, the Grail is the lost and holy vessel whose pursuit is the whole point, glimpsed but rarely grasped.
history: From Old French *graal*, 'cup, dish,' from Medieval Latin *gradalis*. Its sanctity was retro-fitted. In Chrétien de Troyes' *Perceval* the *graal* is a serving-dish carried in procession, not yet holy; only in the reworkings by clerical authors — above all the early-13th-century *Quest of the Holy Grail* — does it become the wonder-working chalice of the Last Supper, thick with theological meaning. The dinner-dish rose into the Holy Grail, and 'grail' now names any sought-after ultimate — the philosophers' stone as 'the Holy Grail of alchemy.'

def_sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages

etymology.origin: Old French graal ‘cup, dish’ ← Medieval Latin gradalis
etymology.note: Chrétien de Troyes’ ‘graal’ was just a serving-dish; only later did it become the Holy Grail. The word’s own sanctity was retro-fitted.
etymology.hook: The Holy Grail started as a dinner plate.

flavor_snippets.html:
"Look deep into the <mark>Grail</mark>, and see there what you desire most."
—Gerda Äagesdotter, Archmage of the Unseen
